parentQuestion: In order for sound to reach the spiral organ (of Corti), the auditory ossicles must vibrate the oval window and set the endolymph in motion.

scaffolds.q: Sound travels as waves through air.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Sound is mechanical vibration that travels through air molecules.

scaffolds.q: The tympanic membrane (eardrum) vibrates when sound waves hit it.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The eardrum converts air pressure waves into mechanical vibration.

scaffolds.q: The three auditory ossicles are the smallest bones in the body.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Malleus, incus, and stapes — tiny bones that amplify sound vibrations.

scaffolds.q: The ossicles connect the tympanic membrane to the oval window.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Eardrum → malleus → incus → stapes → oval window. A chain of transmission.

scaffolds.q: The inner ear is filled with fluid, not air.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The cochlea contains perilymph and endolymph — fluid that carries sound waves.

scaffolds.q: The stapes footplate pushes on the oval window to create fluid waves.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The stapes rocks in the oval window, transmitting vibration to cochlear fluid.

scaffolds.q: The ossicles amplify sound because they concentrate force from a large area to a small area.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Large eardrum → small oval window = ~22x pressure amplification (impedance matching).

scaffolds.q: Fluid waves in the cochlea move the basilar membrane where the organ of Corti sits.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Basilar membrane movement stimulates hair cells in the organ of Corti.

scaffolds.q: Sound reaches the organ of Corti via: Ossicles → oval window → cochlear fluid → basilar membrane.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This chain converts airborne sound to fluid waves to mechanical stimulation of hair cells.
